skill.name: xdrop
skill.description: Secure file transfer skill for uploading and downloading encrypted files through Xdrop servers from the terminal
skill.summary: End-to-end encrypted file sharing via Xdrop servers with CLI automation support
skill.author: xixu-me

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer flagged 155 patterns at risk score 100/100, but most are false positives. The 'backtick execution' findings in SKILL.md are markdown code blocks, not shell execution. Crypto functions use standard Web Crypto API (AES-GCM, HKDF-SHA256) correctly. Network and filesystem access is expected for this file transfer utility. Low risk due to network access and environment variable usage for configuration.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives - Markdown Backticks
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged backticks in SKILL.md as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution', but these are markdown code fence delimiters for documentation examples, not actual shell command execution.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives - Crypto API Misclassification
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged crypto.subtle operations as 'weak cryptographic algorithm', but the code correctly uses AES-GCM-256 with HKDF-SHA256 key derivation, which are industry-standard secure algorithms.

content.user_title: Transfer encrypted files securely via Xdrop servers
content.value_statement: Share files securely with end-to-end encryption using Xdrop servers. This skill automates encrypted file upload and download workflows directly from your terminal with proper key management.
actual_capabilities: Upload files or directories to Xdrop servers with end-to-end AES-GCM encryption
Download and decrypt files from Xdrop share links including key fragments
Automatic key derivation using HKDF-SHA256 for secure key wrapping
Preserves file paths and directory structures during transfer
Handles chunked uploads for large files with configurable concurrency
limitations: Requires Bun runtime environment to execute scripts
Download links must include the full #k=... decryption key fragment
Server must be a compatible Xdrop server with the expected API endpoints
Files are subject to server-configured expiry times and size limits

use_cases.title: Developer sharing build artifacts
use_cases.description: Upload compiled binaries or dist folders to share with team members securely. The --json flag enables CI/CD integration for automated artifact distribution.

use_cases.title: Secure document exchange
use_cases.description: Share sensitive documents with clients or partners using encrypted links that expire automatically. Recipients can download and decrypt without additional tools.

use_cases.title: Automated backup transfers
use_cases.description: Integrate with backup scripts to periodically upload encrypted backups to Xdrop servers. The --quiet flag enables clean script output for logging.

prompt_templates.title: Basic file upload
prompt_templates.prompt: Upload this file to the Xdrop server at https://xdrop.example.com and share the link: ./report.pdf
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick single file upload with default settings

prompt_templates.title: Upload with expiry and JSON output
prompt_templates.prompt: Upload ./archive.zip to the Xdrop server with a 10-minute expiry. Return the result as JSON so I can capture the transferId and shareUrl for my automation script.

prompt_templates.title: Download from share link
prompt_templates.prompt: Download and decrypt the files from this Xdrop link: https://xdrop.example.com/t/abc123#k=base64key... Save them to the ./downloads folder.
prompt_templates.scenario: Receiving encrypted files from a share link

prompt_templates.title: Silent batch upload
prompt_templates.prompt: Upload the entire ./dist directory to https://xdrop.example.com using quiet mode. I need to capture just the share link without progress output.

output_examples.input: Upload a single PDF file with JSON output
Download files from a share link
Upload multiple files with quiet mode

best_practices: Always use --quiet when another command needs to capture stdout for automation
Verify the share link includes the full #k=... fragment before sharing with recipients
Set appropriate --expires-in values based on sensitivity and use case
anti_patterns: Do not share Xdrop links without the decryption key fragment - they cannot be decrypted
Avoid using the default expiry for sensitive files - set explicit --expires-in values
Do not bypass the scripts built-in path sanitization with manual file operations

faq.question: What is Xdrop and how does it work?
faq.answer: Xdrop is a secure file transfer protocol that uses end-to-end encryption. Files are encrypted client-side before upload, and the decryption key is included in the share link fragment. The server never sees the unencrypted content or the full decryption key.

faq.question: Do I need to install any dependencies?
faq.answer: Yes, you need the Bun runtime installed. The scripts use native Node.js modules and the Web Crypto API, which Bun provides. No additional npm packages are required.

faq.question: What happens if I lose the #k=... fragment from the share link?
faq.answer: The link becomes unusable. The decryption key in the fragment is required to unwrap the root key and decrypt the files. Always share the complete link including the hash fragment.

faq.question: Can I upload directories or only individual files?
faq.answer: Both are supported. When you provide a directory path, the script recursively collects all files while preserving the directory structure. The relative paths are maintained in the transfer manifest.

faq.question: How do I integrate this into my CI/CD pipeline?
faq.answer: Use the --json flag to get structured output with transferId, shareUrl, and expiresAt. Combine with --quiet to suppress progress output. Your pipeline can parse the JSON and use the shareUrl for notifications or further processing.

faq.question: What encryption algorithm is used?
faq.answer: Files are encrypted using AES-GCM with 256-bit keys. Keys are derived using HKDF-SHA256. Each file has a unique key derived from the root key, and each chunk uses a unique IV constructed from a nonce prefix and chunk index.
